pages.full_text:
back at the camp. I put in a load of [?washing] + then swam to cool off before [?hanging] the washing.
     Simon + John walked back to Malaucene to buy pâté + cakes for supper. William will wear a skimpy suit but Charles won't.
__Thursday__
     I had a migraine. William + [?Boyony] left. They took their pavilion awning, but Simon put up a good sheet awning. Charles made sandwiches with baguettes for lunch. Then he + John walked into the village. Simon + Eve swam + Simon mended the switch on the tent light.  [annotation in smaller hand:] paper from paper factory powered by water wheel
pages.notes: Page continues from a previous spread (starts mid-sentence). Lower half of page is decorated with pressed flowers and a fern leaf. The name after 'William +' is unclear — could read '[?Boyony]', '[?Yvonne]', or another name; transcribed as best reading '[?Boyony]'. The first word after 'load of' is partially legible — likely 'washing' or 'clothes'; transcribed as '[?washing]'. The word before 'the washing' at the end of line 2 could be 'hanging' or 'banging'. A small annotation in lighter/smaller ink at the bottom of the text reads 'paper from paper factory powered by water wheel'. No year directly on page; context from volume B section label (2005–2008) and France/Provence location. 'Malaucene' is the village of Malaucène in the Vaucluse, Provence, France.

pages.full_text:
We walked into Malaucene again to have supper, in the same restaurant.
__Friday__
     had sun today so cooler, + yippee, the smelly Belges on the pitch next door, who chain smoked, are packing up to leave. We left early (9.15?) to

go to Fontaine-de-Vaucluse, a famous place where a river comes out of a cave, low down. We looked down into the pool, about 20m below where we stood. In the spring, the whole chasm has water flowing up into it, from the spring (with the level of the fig trees) & it
pages.notes: Page contains a glued-in postcard photograph of the spring/cave pool at Fontaine-de-Vaucluse, with printed caption 'Fontaine-de-Vaucluse' at top and 'Le Gouffre' at bottom right. Handwritten text wraps around the left side and continues below the postcard. 'Belges' is French for Belgians. Text cuts off at the bottom of the page (continues on next spread). No year on this page; context from volume B section and the Provence/Vaucluse setting suggests mid-2000s. 'Malaucene' = Malaucène, Vaucluse, France. '(9.15?)' — the question mark appears to be the diarist's own uncertainty about the time.
